What happened?

If one:
1. loads a dataset saved using `engine="h5ncetdf"` with a string coordinate say `<U2`
2. merges it with another dataset which matches but has longer strings in the same coordinate, say `<U4`
3. then saves *that* merged dataset using `engine="h5ncetdf"`
4. then the encoding from loading the initial dataset, which survives the merge, causes the dataset variable to be silently truncated back to "<U2", such that when it is loaded again the data is incorrect.

~~This is specific to the "h5netcdf" engine.~~ This doesn't happen however with the "scipy" engine.

### What did you expect to happen?

I guess the encoding should be dropped or updated during the `merge` call.

### Minimal Complete Verifiable Example

```Python
import xarray as xr

engine = "h5netcdf"

ds1 = xr.Dataset(coords={'x': ['ab', 'bc', 'c']})
ds1.to_netcdf('ds1.h5', engine=engine)
ds1 = xr.open_dataset('ds1.h5', engine=engine)
ds1.close()


ds2 = xr.Dataset(coords={'x': ['abc', 'bcd', 'cd']})
ds2 = ds1.merge(ds2)
print(ds2.x.encoding)
print("expected", ds2.x.values)
ds2.to_netcdf('ds1.h5', engine=engine)

ds2 = xr.open_dataset('ds1.h5', engine=engine)
ds2.close()
print("loaded  ", ds2.x.values)
# {'dtype': dtype('<U2')}
# expected ['ab' 'abc' 'bc' 'bcd' 'c' 'cd']
# loaded   ['ab' 'ab' 'bc' 'bc' 'c' 'cd']
```


### MVCE confirmation

- [X] Minimal example — the example is as focused as reasonably possible to demonstrate the underlying issue in xarray.
- [X] Complete example — the example is self-contained, including all data and the text of any traceback.
- [X] Verifiable example — the example copy & pastes into an IPython prompt or [Binder notebook](https://mybinder.org/v2/gh/pydata/xarray/main?urlpath=lab/tree/doc/examples/blank_template.ipynb), returning the result.
- [X] New issue — a search of GitHub Issues suggests this is not a duplicate.
- [X] Recent environment — the issue occurs with the latest version of xarray and its dependencies.

a given empty dataset with only coordinates

```python
import xarray as xr
import numpy as np
data = xr.Dataset(coords={'x': np.linspace(-1, 1), 'y': np.linspace(0, 10), 'a': 1, 'b': 5})
```

I'd like to iterate over the product of coordinates, in a similar way as it can be done for ``numpy.array``s

```python
data = np.zeros((10, 5, 10))
for (i, j, k), _ in np.ndenumerate(data):
    data[i, j, k] = some_function(i, j, k)
```

to fill the ``data`` with values of some function.

Also I'd like to extend this to the cases of functions that are multi-valued, i.e. they return a ``numpy.array``.

Is there an easy way to do so? I was unable to find anything similar in the {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/23513","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/23513/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/23513/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/23513/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23513","id":1650106664,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAA3dP85NZBjF","number":23513,"title":"ENH: speed up einsum with optimize using batched matmul","user":{"login":"jcmgray","id":8982598,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg5ODI1OTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8982598?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray","html_url":"https://github.com/jcmgray","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359239,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjM5","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/01%20-%20Enhancement","name":"01 - Enhancement","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2023-03-31T22:15:54Z","updated_at":"2023-07-26T05:06:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/23513","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23513","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23513.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23513.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR enables dispatching *all* pairwise calls in `einsum` when `optimize=` is turned on to batched matrix multiply (or non-batched `matmul` and `multiply` as special cases). For a short explanation of the implementation see here - https://github.com/jcmgray/einsum_bmm.

Currently falling back to `c_einsum` for these, when there exist batch indices, induces potentially many orders of magnitude slow downs (#22604, e.g. 7000x), particularly in the case of large or high dimensional tensors.

After fairly extensive benchmarking (below), I *believe* that this should be a uniform improvement across essentially all the cases when one would use `optimize=True`. This is partly enabled by the modern performance of matmul which in the past was not always faster than `einsum`. However, I know `einsum` has an enormous number of uses and I'd gladly accept more suggestions for benchmarks, and would understand people's hesitation! 

(@dgasmith, @seberg)

## Other notes:
- This replaces all the `can_blas` logic and `tensordot` calls, and simplifies everything into the `bmm_einsum`, which encapsulates how `tensordot` is implemented
- When there are no contracted indices, `numpy.multiply` is used for slightly better performance
- The current implementation can simply pass `out` and other kwargs on to `mutiply` and `matmul`, unlike tensordot, meaning `einsum('ij,jk->ik', x, y, optimize=True, out=z)` should write directly to `z`
- The fusing allows `einsum(eq, ..., optimize=True)` to be used when there are more than 32 indices involved (#5744).
- The low overhead of the actuall `bmm_einsum` implementation comes from caching what operations to use based on `(eq, x.shape, y.shape)`, using `@functools.lru_cache(2**12)`.
- the performance broadly speaking is brought in line with `torch.einsum`

## TODO: 
- [ ] update docs?
- [ ] decide cache size?
- [ ] should I lint the existing `einsumfunc.py` code?

## Benchmarks

In the following the kernels are the following:

- `no optimize`: the base `np.einsum(eq, x, y)` i.e. `c_einsum`
- `optimize + dot`: the current `np.einsum(eq, x, y, optimize=True)` which calls `tensordot` where possible, and also induces some overhead from the potential path optimization (even though there are only two terms in the following, the equation must be checked and parsed etc.)
- `optimize + bmm`: the proposed `np.einsum(eq, x, y, optimize=True)` which calls batched matmul, and still induces some overhead from the potential path optimization
- `no optimize + bmm`: this calls the pairwise bmm einsum directly, and is provided simply to show what overhead comes from the bmm impl, and what from the potential path optimization 

The main two to compare are `optimize + dot` to `optimize + bmm`. Overall I'd summarize the results as the following:

1. when a contraction is memory bound, all methods are pretty much equivalent
2. when a contraction involves no batch dimensions, performance of `dot` is retained
3. when a contraction involves batch dimensions, performance can be orders of magnitude faster

All dimensions are size `n` unless otherwise noted.

![vec_inner](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239233-f79f335c-677c-42ad-bb98-ec230ffff4ba.png)

![vec_outer](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239247-47423fbd-5728-4ddb-b318-088e13cc9c3e.png)

![square_matmul](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229245134-4708158d-f566-493c-bdbe-8f6bd2986b98.png)

![square_matvec](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229245235-cf629050-ff83-4f00-ab7c-6087b5c09365.png)


For `batch_matmul_small` the sizes are `(n, 2, 2), (n, 2, 2)`.

![batch_matmul_small](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239252-995bcf6d-15de-476a-80b6-8bb2f0127b3e.png)

![square_vecmat](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239254-3938e60e-837f-452f-961d-6d6b771dbca9.png)

![batch_matmul_equal](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239255-a53912ca-06e3-4949-8d3c-915d344ae549.png)

For `batch_matmul_large` the sizes are `(10, n, n), (10, n, n)`.

![batch_matmul_large](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239257-bdb12726-8b3a-4de6-96f9-af32cd3565d2.png)

![hadamard](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239259-03cb8172-4f69-4127-a888-24da8b324ee9.png)

![hadamard_unalinged](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239261-d2c10947-c2bc-4a92-a12e-fc0f4f22a22c.png)

![CCSDT_1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239262-20e7dce6-bb1f-4226-93f6-2067be2f14e9.png)

![interleaved_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239263-8abb6eb0-cdd5-43e0-a118-51b18e716ca0.png)

![interleaved_batched_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239264-1ef48196-1036-4418-9007-ae583c0d0a3a.png)

In `random_extreme` the shapes are taken as the following:
```python
[
    (n, 4, 3, 4, 3, 4, 2, 4, 2, n, 2, n, 3, 2, 4),
    (2, 4, n, n, 4, 4, 4, 3, 4, 4, 4, 3, 4)
]
```

![random_extreme](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239265-1bf3da61-b35f-4300-8aee-55367d0af183.png)

In `many_small_dims_dot` the shapes are `([2] * n, [2] * n)` and half in an interleaved pattern are contracted.

![many_small_dims_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239268-f90edf60-7c64-4437-807b-48b8004d7710.png)

In `many_small_dims_batched_dot` the shapes are `([2] * n, [2] * n)` and a third in an interleaved pattern are contracted and a third in an interleaved pattern are batch indices.

![many_small_dims_batched_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229245350-91a26a21-82c5-4a5b-aa7b-eff192882ca1.png)

I.e. do we expect there to be cases where the arrays are not contiguous, and we expect it to be faster to leave them so?

My guess is that this is only the case for certain small contractions that are not the focus of when people use `optimize=True`. 

Of course having some rough heuristic in `matmul` itself would be cleaner.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1650987252/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I stumbled on [autoray](https://autoray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html) ([Github](https://github.com/jcmgray/autoray)) by @jcmgray which provides an abstract interface to a number of array types.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/23513","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/23513/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/23513/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/23513/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23513","id":1650106664,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAA3dP85NZBjF","number":23513,"title":"ENH: speed up einsum with optimize using batched matmul","user":{"login":"jcmgray","id":8982598,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg5ODI1OTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8982598?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray","html_url":"https://github.com/jcmgray","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359239,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjM5","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/01%20-%20Enhancement","name":"01 - Enhancement","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-03-31T22:15:54Z","updated_at":"2023-05-06T18:56:50Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/23513","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23513","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23513.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23513.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR enables dispatching *all* pairwise calls in `einsum` when `optimize=` is turned on to batched matrix multiply (or non-batched `matmul` and `multiply` as special cases). For a short explanation of the implementation see here - https://github.com/jcmgray/einsum_bmm.

Currently falling back to `c_einsum` for these, when there exist batch indices, induces potentially many orders of magnitude slow downs (#22604, e.g. 7000x), particularly in the case of large or high dimensional tensors.

After fairly extensive benchmarking (below), I *believe* that this should be a uniform improvement across essentially all the cases when one would use `optimize=True`. This is partly enabled by the modern performance of matmul which in the past was not always faster than `einsum`. However, I know `einsum` has an enormous number of uses and I'd gladly accept more suggestions for benchmarks, and would understand people's hesitation! 

(@dgasmith, @seberg)

## Other notes:
- This replaces all the `can_blas` logic and `tensordot` calls, and simplifies everything into the `bmm_einsum`, which encapsulates how `tensordot` is implemented
- When there are no contracted indices, `numpy.multiply` is used for slightly better performance
- The current implementation can simply pass `out` and other kwargs on to `mutiply` and `matmul`, unlike tensordot, meaning `einsum('ij,jk->ik', x, y, optimize=True, out=z)` should write directly to `z`
- The fusing allows `einsum(eq, ..., optimize=True)` to be used when there are more than 32 indices involved (#5744).
- The low overhead of the actuall `bmm_einsum` implementation comes from caching what operations to use based on `(eq, x.shape, y.shape)`, using `@functools.lru_cache(2**12)`.
- the performance broadly speaking is brought in line with `torch.einsum`

## TODO: 
- [ ] update docs?
- [ ] decide cache size?
- [ ] should I lint the existing `einsumfunc.py` code?

## Benchmarks

In the following the kernels are the following:

- `no optimize`: the base `np.einsum(eq, x, y)` i.e. `c_einsum`
- `optimize + dot`: the current `np.einsum(eq, x, y, optimize=True)` which calls `tensordot` where possible, and also induces some overhead from the potential path optimization (even though there are only two terms in the following, the equation must be checked and parsed etc.)
- `optimize + bmm`: the proposed `np.einsum(eq, x, y, optimize=True)` which calls batched matmul, and still induces some overhead from the potential path optimization
- `no optimize + bmm`: this calls the pairwise bmm einsum directly, and is provided simply to show what overhead comes from the bmm impl, and what from the potential path optimization 

The main two to compare are `optimize + dot` to `optimize + bmm`. Overall I'd summarize the results as the following:

1. when a contraction is memory bound, all methods are pretty much equivalent
2. when a contraction involves no batch dimensions, performance of `dot` is retained
3. when a contraction involves batch dimensions, performance can be orders of magnitude faster

All dimensions are size `n` unless otherwise noted.

![vec_inner](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239233-f79f335c-677c-42ad-bb98-ec230ffff4ba.png)

![vec_outer](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239247-47423fbd-5728-4ddb-b318-088e13cc9c3e.png)

![square_matmul](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229245134-4708158d-f566-493c-bdbe-8f6bd2986b98.png)

![square_matvec](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229245235-cf629050-ff83-4f00-ab7c-6087b5c09365.png)


For `batch_matmul_small` the sizes are `(n, 2, 2), (n, 2, 2)`.

![batch_matmul_small](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239252-995bcf6d-15de-476a-80b6-8bb2f0127b3e.png)

![square_vecmat](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239254-3938e60e-837f-452f-961d-6d6b771dbca9.png)

![batch_matmul_equal](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239255-a53912ca-06e3-4949-8d3c-915d344ae549.png)

For `batch_matmul_large` the sizes are `(10, n, n), (10, n, n)`.

![batch_matmul_large](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239257-bdb12726-8b3a-4de6-96f9-af32cd3565d2.png)

![hadamard](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239259-03cb8172-4f69-4127-a888-24da8b324ee9.png)

![hadamard_unalinged](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239261-d2c10947-c2bc-4a92-a12e-fc0f4f22a22c.png)

![CCSDT_1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239262-20e7dce6-bb1f-4226-93f6-2067be2f14e9.png)

![interleaved_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239263-8abb6eb0-cdd5-43e0-a118-51b18e716ca0.png)

![interleaved_batched_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239264-1ef48196-1036-4418-9007-ae583c0d0a3a.png)

In `random_extreme` the shapes are taken as the following:
```python
[
    (n, 4, 3, 4, 3, 4, 2, 4, 2, n, 2, n, 3, 2, 4),
    (2, 4, n, n, 4, 4, 4, 3, 4, 4, 4, 3, 4)
]
```

![random_extreme](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239265-1bf3da61-b35f-4300-8aee-55367d0af183.png)

In `many_small_dims_dot` the shapes are `([2] * n, [2] * n)` and half in an interleaved pattern are contracted.

![many_small_dims_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239268-f90edf60-7c64-4437-807b-48b8004d7710.png)

In `many_small_dims_batched_dot` the shapes are `([2] * n, [2] * n)` and a third in an interleaved pattern are contracted and a third in an interleaved pattern are batch indices.

![many_small_dims_batched_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229245350-91a26a21-82c5-4a5b-aa7b-eff192882ca1.png)

- `optimize + dot`: the current `np.einsum(eq, x, y, optimize=True)` which calls `tensordot` where possible, and also induces some overhead from the potential path optimization (even though there are only two terms in the following, the equation must be checked and parsed etc.)
- `optimize + bmm`: the proposed `np.einsum(eq, x, y, optimize=True)` which calls batched matmul, and still induces some overhead from the potential path optimization
- `no optimize + bmm`: this calls the pairwise bmm einsum directly, and is provided simply to show what overhead comes from the bmm impl, and what from the potential path optimization 

The main two to compare are `optimize + dot` to `optimize + bmm`. Overall I'd summarize the results as the following:

1. when a contraction is memory bound, all methods are pretty much equivalent
2. when a contraction involves no batch dimensions, performance of `dot` is retained
3. when a contraction involves batch dimensions, performance can be orders of magnitude faster

All dimensions are size `n` unless otherwise noted.

![vec_inner](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239233-f79f335c-677c-42ad-bb98-ec230ffff4ba.png)

![vec_outer](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239247-47423fbd-5728-4ddb-b318-088e13cc9c3e.png)

![square_matmul](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229245134-4708158d-f566-493c-bdbe-8f6bd2986b98.png)

![square_matvec](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229245235-cf629050-ff83-4f00-ab7c-6087b5c09365.png)


For `batch_matmul_small` the sizes are `(n, 2, 2), (n, 2, 2)`.

![batch_matmul_small](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239252-995bcf6d-15de-476a-80b6-8bb2f0127b3e.png)

![square_vecmat](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239254-3938e60e-837f-452f-961d-6d6b771dbca9.png)

![batch_matmul_equal](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239255-a53912ca-06e3-4949-8d3c-915d344ae549.png)

For `batch_matmul_large` the sizes are `(10, n, n), (10, n, n)`.

![batch_matmul_large](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239257-bdb12726-8b3a-4de6-96f9-af32cd3565d2.png)

![hadamard](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239259-03cb8172-4f69-4127-a888-24da8b324ee9.png)

![hadamard_unalinged](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239261-d2c10947-c2bc-4a92-a12e-fc0f4f22a22c.png)

![CCSDT_1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239262-20e7dce6-bb1f-4226-93f6-2067be2f14e9.png)

![interleaved_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239263-8abb6eb0-cdd5-43e0-a118-51b18e716ca0.png)

![interleaved_batched_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239264-1ef48196-1036-4418-9007-ae583c0d0a3a.png)

In `random_extreme` the shapes are taken as the following:
```python
[
    (n, 4, 3, 4, 3, 4, 2, 4, 2, n, 2, n, 3, 2, 4),
    (2, 4, n, n, 4, 4, 4, 3, 4, 4, 4, 3, 4)
]
```

![random_extreme](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239265-1bf3da61-b35f-4300-8aee-55367d0af183.png)

In `many_small_dims_dot` the shapes are `([2] * n, [2] * n)` and half in an interleaved pattern are contracted.

![many_small_dims_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239268-f90edf60-7c64-4437-807b-48b8004d7710.png)

In `many_small_dims_batched_dot` the shapes are `([2] * n, [2] * n)` and a third in an interleaved pattern are contracted and a third in an interleaved pattern are batch indices.

![many_small_dims_batched_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229245350-91a26a21-82c5-4a5b-aa7b-eff192882ca1.png)

```python
import numpy as np
d = 1000
x = np.random.randn(d, d)
y = np.random.randn(d, d) + 1j * np.random.randn(d, d)

%%timeit
z = np.einsum('ij,jk->ik', x, y.real, optimize=True)
# -->
# 11.5 ms ± 473 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)  (current dot backend)
# -->
# 897 ms ± 23.7 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)  (new matmul backend)
```

If the solution in #23752 is to do the copy when needed then that is very much aligned with the logic of `optimize=True`, i.e. trade memory and copying for being able to BLAS-it. Else one could check in the einsum_bmm call but preferably it would be deferred to `matmul` I suppose.

### Issue

`blueqat` depends on `autoray` and currently the `pip check` fails with the error message: 
```
blueqat 2.0.4 has requirement autoray>=0.2.5, but you have autoray 0.0.0.
```
So it seems the version from `pypi` is not transferred correctly.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/sparse/issues/582","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/sparse","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/sparse/issues/582/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/sparse/issues/582/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/sparse/issues/582/events","html_url":"https://github.com/pydata/sparse/issues/582","id":1604250787,"node_id":"I_kwDOBUVI8s5fnuyj","number":582,"title":"Difference in sparse.einsum and np.einsum behaviour","user":{"login":"kumgleb","id":68147941,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTQ3OTQx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68147941?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kumgleb","html_url":"https://github.com/kumgleb","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kumgleb/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kumgleb/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kumgleb/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kumgleb/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kumgleb/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kumgleb/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kumgleb/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kumgleb/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kumgleb/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":584971915,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw1ODQ5NzE5MTU=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/sparse/labels/type:support","name":"type:support","color":"e8a58f","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2023-03-01T05:04:23Z","updated_at":"2023-03-01T05:39:38Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"First of all thanks for `einsum` implementation in `sparse`!

I faced with a problem using it in my project, the problem is follows:
* I need to use `einsum` without implicit subscripts, here is a simple example in `numpy`:
```
v1 = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
v2 = [0, 1]

values = np.einsum(v1, v2)
print(values)
>>> array([[1, 2],
           [3, 4]])
```

* If I try the same in `sparse` I get an error:
```
v1 = sparse.COO(np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4]]))
v2 = sparse.COO(np.array([0, 1]))

values = sparse.einsum(v1, v2)
values
>>> UFuncTypeError: ufunc 'equal' did not contain a loop with signature matching types (<class 'numpy.dtype[int64]'>, <class 'numpy.dtype[str_]'>) -> None
```

* But if I provide subscripts it works fine:
```
v1 = sparse.COO(np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4]]))
v2 = sparse.COO(np.array([0, 1]))

values = sparse.einsum('ab->ab', v1, v2)
print(values.todense())
>>> array([[1, 2],
           [3, 4]])
```

So my question is: is it possible to avoid subscripts in `sparse.einsum` in the same way as in `numpy.einsum`?
---
sparse version 0.14.0
numpy version 1.23.1

* implicit output: `einsum('ab', x)` - i.e. the output is not given but automatically computed, this is handled already
* interleaved format: `einsum(array_0, indices_0, array_1, indices_1, ...[, output_indices])`, this is not supported yet, but is just a matter of parsing that could be lifted from e.g. `opt_einsum`.

Note that `einsum('ab->ab, v1, v2)` is not actually valid! There should only be one term, and that last call should probably error, as it does with `numpy`...","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/sparse/issues/comments/1449381280/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Question","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"Question about NumPy usage or development"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":10,"created_at":"2022-11-16T16:55:54Z","updated_at":"2023-03-29T05:40:51Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

`np.einsum` is ~20x slower than other libraries. 

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
x = np.random.uniform(size=(1000, 1, 500))
y = np.random.uniform(size=(1000, 1, 500))
%timeit np.einsum('thd,Thd->thT', x, y)
363 ms ± 2.21 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)


import jax.numpy as jnp
%timeit jnp.einsum('thd,Thd->thT', x, y)
21.2 ms ± 1.84 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)


import torch
x = torch.rand(size=(1000, 1, 500))
y = torch.rand(size=(1000, 1, 500))
%timeit torch.einsum('thd,Thd->thT', x, y)
14 ms ± 87.7 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)


import tensorflow as tf
x = tf.random.uniform(shape=(1000, 1, 500))
y = tf.random.uniform(shape=(1000, 1, 500))
%timeit tf.einsum('thd,Thd->thT', x, y)
18.7 ms ± 109 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)
```


### Error message:

_No response_

### NumPy/Python version information:

1.21.6 3.7.15 (default, Oct 12 2022, 19:14:55)

_No response_","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/22604/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/22604/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1487976946","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/22604#issuecomment-1487976946","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/22604","id":1487976946,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP85YsLny","user":{"login":"jcmgray","id":8982598,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg5ODI1OTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8982598?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray","html_url":"https://github.com/jcmgray","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-03-29T05:40:50Z","updated_at":"2023-03-29T05:40:50Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"I have an implementation here - https://github.com/jcmgray/einsum_bmm - of `einsum`, based on batch matrix multiplication, that can be dramatically faster (100 to even 1000x) in certain cases I am interested in (large and high dimensional tensors).

For `numpy` and this contraction e.g.:
```python
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
x = rng.uniform(size=(10_000, 1, 500))
y = rng.uniform(size=(10_000, 1, 500))
np.einsum('thd,Thd->thT', x, y, optimize=True)
```

it goes from `18sec -> 500ms` for me. Part of that is the multithreading, but also simply that matrix multiplication is so heavily optimized already.

Notes:
* when `optimize=True` and there are no 'batch indices' in a particular contraction, then pure matmul is already used (via tensordot).
* This just speeds up *pairwise* contractions, since you would always want to do some sort of pairwise optimization (the other component of what `optimize=True` does, and `opt_einsum` etc)
* for small tensors the current numpy c_einsum is still very low overhead and slightly faster.

---

A) if there is interest I'd be happy to put a PR together for `numpy.einsum` I guess simply using this when `optimize=True`
B) I've tried many different contractions, but there are almost infinitely many edge cases with `einsum`, would be interested if anyone has specific test cases to benchmark!

And agreed on keeping `optimize=False` as the default to prioritize low-overhead small contractions. I think the path finding overhead adds minimum a few 10s of us generally. One might want to special case 2 terms and `optimize=True` to avoid the path finding machinery at all.

I did run some new tests on those specific examples (using c_einsum directly):
```python
eq = 'i,i->'
shapes = [(3,), (3,)]

%%timeit x, y = [np.random.randn(*shape) for shape in shapes]
z = c_einsum(eq, x, y)
# 841 ns ± 8.66 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1,000,000 loops each)

%%timeit x, y = [np.random.randn(*shape) for shape in shapes]
z = einsum_via_bmm(eq, x, y)
# 2.13 µs ± 19 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100,000 loops each)
```
so it seems the overhead of just the contraction is ~1us in what is practically the smallest test case. I'd note that the low overhead is only possible if you are happy to cache what operations to use based on `eq, a.shape, b.shape`, which in my opinion is very reasonable, but I don't know if `numpy` generally avoids caching.

As the sizes increase I haven't yet found a case where the batched matmul approach is not asymptotically better yet however, including that second example:
```python
eq = 'ijk,ikl->ijl'
shapes = [(10000, 2, 2), (10000, 2, 2)]

%%timeit x, y = [np.random.randn(*shape) for shape in shapes]
z = c_einsum(eq, x, y)
# 2.02 ms ± 29.8 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)

%%timeit x, y = [np.random.randn(*shape) for shape in shapes]
z = einsum_via_bmm(eq, x, y)
# 1.55 ms ± 14.3 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1,000 loops each)

### Reproduce the code example:

y = np.random.uniform(size=(1000, 1, 500))
%timeit np.einsum('thd,Thd->thT', x, y)
363 ms ± 2.21 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)


import jax.numpy as jnp
%timeit jnp.einsum('thd,Thd->thT', x, y)
21.2 ms ± 1.84 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)


import torch
x = torch.rand(size=(1000, 1, 500))
y = torch.rand(size=(1000, 1, 500))
%timeit torch.einsum('thd,Thd->thT', x, y)
14 ms ± 87.7 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)


import tensorflow as tf
x = tf.random.uniform(shape=(1000, 1, 500))
y = tf.random.uniform(shape=(1000, 1, 500))
%timeit tf.einsum('thd,Thd->thT', x, y)
18.7 ms ± 109 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)

In my opinion there are not many use cases where you would turn on `optimize` but *not* for batch contractions specifically, but happy to hear objections! 

I think, with the modern performance of `matmul`, this is pretty much a uniform improvement across use-cases, apart from very small tensors when you'd keep `optimize=False` anyway.

> Matmul is a nice example of how to optimize memory access by blocked iteration, that may be very different from what is here, but doing blocked iteration might drastically speed up the current einsum C code (and many other operations).

Thanks for the explanation!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/1491106961/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":23513,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/23513","id":1298405573,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAA3dP85NZBjF","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23513","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23513.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/23513.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/23513","number":23513,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"ENH: speed up einsum with optimize using batched matmul","user":{"login":"jcmgray","id":8982598,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg5ODI1OTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8982598?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray","html_url":"https://github.com/jcmgray","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This PR enables dispatching *all* pairwise calls in `einsum` when `optimize=` is turned on to batched matrix multiply (or non-batched `matmul` and `multiply` as special cases). For a short explanation of the implementation see here - https://github.com/jcmgray/einsum_bmm.

Currently falling back to `c_einsum` for these, when there exist batch indices, induces potentially many orders of magnitude slow downs (#22604, e.g. 7000x), particularly in the case of large or high dimensional tensors.

After fairly extensive benchmarking (below), I *believe* that this should be a uniform improvement across essentially all the cases when one would use `optimize=True`. This is partly enabled by the modern performance of matmul which in the past was not always faster than `einsum`. However, I know `einsum` has an enormous number of uses and I'd gladly accept more suggestions for benchmarks, and would understand people's hesitation! 

(@dgasmith, @seberg)

## Other notes:
- This replaces all the `can_blas` logic and `tensordot` calls, and simplifies everything into the `bmm_einsum`, which encapsulates how `tensordot` is implemented
- When there are no contracted indices, `numpy.multiply` is used for slightly better performance
- The current implementation can simply pass `out` and other kwargs on to `mutiply` and `matmul`, unlike tensordot, meaning `einsum('ij,jk->ik', x, y, optimize=True, out=z)` should write directly to `z`
- The fusing allows `einsum(eq, ..., optimize=True)` to be used when there are more than 32 indices involved (#5744).
- The low overhead of the actuall `bmm_einsum` implementation comes from caching what operations to use based on `(eq, x.shape, y.shape)`, using `@functools.lru_cache(2**12)`.
- the performance broadly speaking is brought in line with `torch.einsum`

## TODO: 
- [ ] update docs?
- [ ] decide cache size?

## Benchmarks

In the following the kernels are the following:

- `no optimize`: the base `np.einsum(eq, x, y)` i.e. `c_einsum`
- `optimize + dot`: the current `np.einsum(eq, x, y, optimize=True)` which calls `tensordot` where possible, and also induces some overhead from the potential path optimization (even though there are only two terms in the following, the equation must be checked and parsed etc.)
- `optimize + bmm`: the proposed `np.einsum(eq, x, y, optimize=True)` which calls batched matmul, and still induces some overhead from the potential path optimization
- `no optimize + bmm`: this calls the pairwise bmm einsum directly, and is provided simply to show what overhead comes from the bmm impl, and what from the potential path optimization 

The main two to compare are `optimize + dot` to `optimize + bmm`. Overall I'd summarize the results as the following:

1. when a contraction is memory bound, all methods are pretty much equivalent
2. when a contraction involves no batch dimensions, performance of `dot` is retained
3. when a contraction involves batch dimensions, performance can be orders of magnitude faster

All dimensions are size `n` unless otherwise noted.

![vec_inner](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239233-f79f335c-677c-42ad-bb98-ec230ffff4ba.png)

![vec_outer](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239247-47423fbd-5728-4ddb-b318-088e13cc9c3e.png)

![square_matmul](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239250-c6e233a0-47fa-4156-aac8-f6d90be119d7.png)

![square_matvec](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239251-b15fcc7f-c3ab-454c-9e0a-63c1d3e6c3bc.png)


For `batch_matmul_small` the sizes are `(n, 2, 2), (n, 2, 2)`.

![batch_matmul_small](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239252-995bcf6d-15de-476a-80b6-8bb2f0127b3e.png)

![square_vecmat](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239254-3938e60e-837f-452f-961d-6d6b771dbca9.png)

![batch_matmul_equal](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239255-a53912ca-06e3-4949-8d3c-915d344ae549.png)

For `batch_matmul_large` the sizes are `(10, n, n), (10, n, n)`.

![batch_matmul_large](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239257-bdb12726-8b3a-4de6-96f9-af32cd3565d2.png)

![hadamard](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239259-03cb8172-4f69-4127-a888-24da8b324ee9.png)

![hadamard_unalinged](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239261-d2c10947-c2bc-4a92-a12e-fc0f4f22a22c.png)

![CCSDT_1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239262-20e7dce6-bb1f-4226-93f6-2067be2f14e9.png)

![interleaved_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239263-8abb6eb0-cdd5-43e0-a118-51b18e716ca0.png)

![interleaved_batched_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239264-1ef48196-1036-4418-9007-ae583c0d0a3a.png)

In `random_extreme` the shapes are taken as the following:
```python
[
    (n, 4, 3, 4, 3, 4, 2, 4, 2, n, 2, n, 3, 2, 4),
    (2, 4, n, n, 4, 4, 4, 3, 4, 4, 4, 3, 4)
]
```

![random_extreme](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239265-1bf3da61-b35f-4300-8aee-55367d0af183.png)

In `many_small_dims_dot` the shapes are `([2] * n, [2] * n)` and half in an interleaved pattern are contracted.

![many_small_dims_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239268-f90edf60-7c64-4437-807b-48b8004d7710.png)

In `many_small_dims_batched_dot` the shapes are `([2] * n, [2] * n)` and a third in an interleaved pattern are contracted and a third in an interleaved pattern are batch indices.

![many_small_dims_batched_dot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8982598/229239267-191d84f5-4eec-4ef0-95d8-b870a916057f.png)

`np.einsum` is ~20x slower than other libraries. 

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
x = np.random.uniform(size=(1000, 1, 500))
y = np.random.uniform(size=(1000, 1, 500))
%timeit np.einsum('thd,Thd->thT', x, y)
363 ms ± 2.21 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)


import jax.numpy as jnp
%timeit jnp.einsum('thd,Thd->thT', x, y)
21.2 ms ± 1.84 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)


import torch
x = torch.rand(size=(1000, 1, 500))
y = torch.rand(size=(1000, 1, 500))
%timeit torch.einsum('thd,Thd->thT', x, y)
14 ms ± 87.7 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)


import tensorflow as tf
x = tf.random.uniform(shape=(1000, 1, 500))
y = tf.random.uniform(shape=(1000, 1, 500))
%timeit tf.einsum('thd,Thd->thT', x, y)
18.7 ms ± 109 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)
```

is a general einsum implementation (#31) for the COO format. An implementation was provided in #31 but it doesn't seem to work for all inputs. The implementation here passes all of `opt_einsum`'s test suite edges cases, and is also reasonably performant (e.g. same order of magnitude speed as `tensordot` for cases where they overlap in functionality, sometimes faster). But certainly not the most optimized one could do. One would also probably want to use `opt_einsum` for more than 2 terms generally, but it's quite useful to have a reference, non-optimized form.

### TODO/questions:

### Maybe later:
- [ ] dispatch to tensordot when compatible?
- [ ] benchmarks

### DONE

- [x] main docs?
- [x] main tests
- [x] input checks (such as default fill value?)
- [x] decide what to do with mixed input types?
- [x] edge case tests such as mixed input types?
- [x] docstrings

osx-arm64","user":{"login":"jcmgray","id":8982598,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg5ODI1OTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8982598?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray","html_url":"https://github.com/jcmgray","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":379664686,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzk2NjQ2ODY=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/numpy-feedstock/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"ee0701","default":true,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-01-17T02:53:20Z","updated_at":"2023-01-17T02:53:20Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

On osx-arm64 with numpy==1.24 installed from conda-forge, `np.linalg.eigh` produces incorrect results for complex data types:

```python
import numpy as np

d = 3
A = np.random.randn(d, d) + 1j * np.random.randn(d, d)
A = (A + A.conj().T)

el, ev = np.linalg.eigh(A)

# following should all be close to zero
print(np.linalg.norm(np.identity(d) - (ev @ ev.conj().T)))
# 4.115852147734368
print(np.linalg.norm(np.identity(d) - (ev.conj().T @ ev)))
# 4.115852147734368
print(np.linalg.norm(A - (ev @ np.diag(el) @ ev.conj().T)))
# 11.555496471473255
```

Other linalg such as svd appears to be working (though I haven't checked exhaustively).

I have checked this with:
- `blas=*=acclerate`
- `blas=*=netlib`

(Can't check with `blas=*=openblas` as this doesn't appear to work more generally)

Using `numpy` from pypi the code is working fine. Thus raising the issue here assuming it is something to do with the linked lapack, but apologies if this should be in the main `numpy` repo.


### Installed packages

```shell
# packages in environment at /Users/johnnie/conda/envs/numpy-latest:
#
# Name                    Version                   Build  Channel

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

On osx-arm64 with numpy==1.24 installed from conda-forge, `np.linalg.eigh` produces incorrect results for complex data types:

```python
import numpy as np

d = 3
A = np.random.randn(d, d) + 1j * np.random.randn(d, d)
A = (A + A.conj().T)

el, ev = np.linalg.eigh(A)

# following should all be close to zero
print(np.linalg.norm(np.identity(d) - (ev @ ev.conj().T)))
# 4.115852147734368
print(np.linalg.norm(np.identity(d) - (ev.conj().T @ ev)))
# 4.115852147734368
print(np.linalg.norm(A - (ev @ np.diag(el) @ ev.conj().T)))
# 11.555496471473255
```

Other linalg such as svd appears to be working (though I haven't checked exhaustively).

I have checked this with:
- `blas=*=acclerate`
- `blas=*=netlib`

(Can't check with `blas=*=openblas` as this doesn't appear to work more generally)

Using `numpy` from pypi the code is working fine. Thus raising the issue here assuming it is something to do with the linked lapack, but apologies if this should be in the main `numpy` repo.

Possibly related to [#21950](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/21950), thought the issue here is not simply normalization of the eigenvectors.

An implementation was provided in #31 but it doesn't seem to work for all inputs. The implementation here passes all of `opt_einsum`'s test suite edges cases:
```python
from opt_einsum.tests.test_contract import tests
import numpy as np
import sparse
from sparse.einsum import einsum

d = 4
density = 1.0
for eq in tests:

    terms = eq.split('->')[0].split(',')
    arrays = [
        sparse.random((d,) * len(term), density=density) 
        for term in terms
    ]
    
    sef = einsum(eq, *arrays)
    sn = np.einsum(eq, *(s.todense() for s in arrays))

    e = np.linalg.norm(sef.todense() - sn)
    print(f'{eq:>40}, {e}')
```
```bash
                                   a,->a, 0.0
                                 ab,->ab, 0.0
                                ,ab,->ab, 0.0
                                    ,,->, 0.0

and is also reasonably performant (e.g. same order of magnitude speed as `tensordot` for cases where they overlap in functionality, sometimes faster). But certainly not the most optimized one could do. One would also probably want to use `opt_einsum` for more than 2 terms generally, but it's quite useful to have a reference, non-optimized form.

+def _einsum_single(lhs, rhs, operand):
+    """Perform a single term einsum, i.e. any combination of transposes, sums
+    and traces of dimensions.
+
+    Parameters
+    ----------
+    lhs : str
+        The indices of the input array.
+    rhs : str
+        The indices of the output array.
+    operand : SparseArray
+        The array to perform the einsum on.
+
+    Returns
+    -------
+    output : SparseArray
+    """
+    operand = asCOO(operand)
+
+    if lhs == rhs:
+        # XXX: take copy?
+        return operand
+
+    # check if repeated / 'trace' indices mean we are only taking a subset
+    where = {}
+    for i, ix in enumerate(lhs):
+        where.setdefault(ix, []).append(i)
+
+    selector = None
+    for ix, locs in where.items():
+        loc0, *rlocs = locs
+        if rlocs:
+            # repeated index - only select data where all indices match
+            loc0, *locs = locs
+            subselector = (operand.coords[loc0] == operand.coords[rlocs]).all(axis=0)
+            if selector is None:
+                selector = subselector
+            else:
+                selector &= subselector
+
+    # indices that are removed (i.e. not in the output / `perm`)
+    # are handled by `has_duplicates=True` below
+    perm = [lhs.index(ix) for ix in rhs]
+    new_shape = tuple(operand.shape[i] for i in perm)
+
+    # select the new COO data
+    if selector is not None:
+        new_coords = operand.coords[:, selector][perm]
+        new_data = operand.data[selector]
+    else:
+        new_coords = operand.coords[perm]
+        new_data = operand.data
+
+    if not rhs:
+        # scalar output - match numpy behaviour by not wrapping as array
+        return new_data.sum()
+
+    return operand.__class__(new_coords, new_data, shape=new_shape, has_duplicates=True)","path":"sparse/_common.py","position":72,"original_position":72,"commit_id":"b9a163293ef85cae9fb01a74828049c89454deb1","original_commit_id":"b9a163293ef85cae9fb01a74828049c89454deb1","user":{"login":"jcmgray","id":8982598,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg5ODI1OTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8982598?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray","html_url":"https://github.com/jcmgray","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It is a general einsum implementation (#31) for the COO format. An implementation was provided in #31 but it doesn't seem to work for all inputs. The implementation here passes all of `opt_einsum`'s test suite edges cases, and is also reasonably performant (e.g. same order of magnitude speed as `tensordot` for cases where they overlap in functionality, sometimes faster). But certainly not the most optimized one could do. One would also probably want to use `opt_einsum` for more than 2 terms generally, but it's quite useful to have a reference, non-optimized form.

### TODO/questions:
- [ ] main docs?
- [ ] input checks (such as default fill value?)
- [ ] decide what to do with mixed input types?
- [ ] edge case tests such as mixed input types?

### Maybe later:
- [ ] dispatch to tensordot when compatible?
- [ ] benchmarks

### DONE
- [x] main tests
- [x] docstrings
- [x] decide what to do in the scalar output case: return scalar or sparse? : numpy returns scalar, this is generally convenient ","user":{"login":"jcmgray","id":8982598,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg5ODI1OTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8982598?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray","html_url":"https://github.com/jcmgray","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jcmgray/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is a general einsum implementation (#31) for the COO format. An implementation was provided in #31 but it doesn't seem to work for all inputs. The implementation here passes all of `opt_einsum`'s test suite edges cases, and is also reasonably performant (e.g. same order of magnitude speed as `tensordot` for cases where they overlap in functionality, sometimes faster). But certainly not the most optimized one could do. One would also probably want to use `opt_einsum` for more than 2 terms generally, but it's quite useful to have a reference, non-optimized form.

### TODO/questions:
- [ ] main docs?
- [ ] input checks (such as default fill value?)
- [ ] decide what to do with mixed input types?
- [ ] edge case tests such as mixed input types?

### Maybe later:
- [ ] dispatch to tensordot when compatible?
- [ ] benchmarks

### DONE
- [x] main tests
- [x] docstrings
- [x] decide what to do in the scalar output case: return scalar or sparse? : numpy returns scalar, this is generally convenient +import pytest
+import numpy as np
+import sparse
+
+
+einsum_cases = [
+    "a,->a",

+    ",ab,->ab",
+    ",,->",
+    "a,ab,abc->abc",

+    "bcb,bb,fc,fff->",

+]
+
+
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("subscripts", einsum_cases)
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("density", [0.1, 1.0])
+def test_einsum(subscripts, density):
+    d = 4
+
+    terms = subscripts.split("->")[0].split(",")
+    arrays = [sparse.random((d,) * len(term), density=density) for term in terms]
+    sparse_out = sparse.einsum(subscripts, *arrays)
+    numpy_out = np.einsum(subscripts, *(s.todense() for s in arrays))
+
+    if hasattr(sparse_out, "todense"):

is a general einsum implementation (#31) for the COO format. An implementation was provided in #31 but it doesn't seem to work for all inputs. The implementation here passes all of `opt_einsum`'s test suite edges cases, and is also reasonably performant (e.g. same order of magnitude speed as `tensordot` for cases where they overlap in functionality, sometimes faster). But certainly not the most optimized one could do. One would also probably want to use `opt_einsum` for more than 2 terms generally, but it's quite useful to have a reference, non-optimized form.

is a general einsum implementation (#31) for the COO format. An implementation was provided in #31 but it doesn't seem to work for all inputs. The implementation here passes all of `opt_einsum`'s test suite edges cases, and is also reasonably performant (e.g. same order of magnitude speed as `tensordot` for cases where they overlap in functionality, sometimes faster). But certainly not the most optimized one could do. One would also probably want to use `opt_einsum` for more than 2 terms generally, but it's quite useful to have a reference, non-optimized form.

I've mentioned it alongside tensordot, but let me know if there should more extensive docs. I'm partly assuming the implicit "it's numpy.einsum but for sparse arrays" is enough.

> Shape checks and zero fill value are a must.
> General convention is to return COO if mixed, but the same type if others.
> Yes, please. I've noticed bugs sometimes pop up in this area.

I've added various input and shape checks. The logic of what format to output is already largely handled by the `multiply(*prepared_operands)`. The only non-trivial thing is that `DOK` didn't support `reshape`, and it was cleaner to add it rather than add logic about converting to COO and back etc. But maybe `DOK.reshape` was intentionally not implemented?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pydata/sparse/issues/comments/1321447466/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
